question: You know it well (1)/ that your success in life (2)/ depends not on my advice (3)/ but on somebody else. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'somebody else' ke baad 's lagega — 'somebody else's' (advice). Rule: 'else' ke saath 's ka prayog hota hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He informed me (1)/ that he had gone to (2)/ one of the Minister's residences (3)/ and stayed there all night. (4)
solution: No error. 'one of the Minister's residences' sahi possessive hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The building's roof (1)/ needs repairing otherwise (2)/ it may fall down and (3)/ result into many persons' death. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'result into' idiom galat hai — 'result in' hona chahiye.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I visited (1)/ John's and Mary's house (2)/ twice but (3)/ found the couple absent. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: couple ka ek shared ghar hai — joint possession, sirf last noun par 's: 'John and Mary's house'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: A ton's weight (1)/ is too heavy for (2)/ anyone to (3)/ carry on his head. (4)
solution: No error. 'A ton's weight' (measure + 's, jaise 'a day's work') sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I stopped (1)/ his going out late at night (2)/ because the situation (3)/ in the town was very tense. (4)
solution: No error. Gerund 'going' se pehle possessive 'his' sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He did not approve of (1)/ his son raising stupid (2)/ questions and thereby (3)/ vexing the teacher. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: gerund 'raising' se pehle possessive chahiye — 'his son's raising'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: America's problems (1)/ are, however, (2)/ not so serious (3)/ and awful as India. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: tulna India ke problems se ho rahi hai — 'as India's' (India's problems).
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Your tone of speaking (1)/ as well as your friends (2)/ is really extremely sarcastic (3)/ yet we never take it seriously. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: 'your tone' ki tulna 'your friend's (tone)' se hai — possessive 'your friend's'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The Finance Minister (1)/ boasts of improving (2)/ the economic condition of (3)/ the country's. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'condition of the country' — 'the country's' ki jagah 'the country'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The life of the rich (1)/ is not in many (2)/ ways as peaceful (3)/ as that of the poor's. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'that of the poor' pehle se possessive hai — 'poor's' redundant.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: For your sake (1)/ as well as for your wife (2)/ we all wish (3)/ that you may get that job. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: 'for your sake' ke parallel 'for your wife's (sake)' chahiye.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The expert mason reported to (1)/ the owner of the hotel (2)/ that there was no question (3)/ of the wall's falling down. (4)
solution: No error. Gerund 'falling' se pehle 'wall's' sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The officer said (1)/ that he appreciated his staffs (2)/ coming on time (3)/ and co-operating him. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: 'staff' collective noun hai, iska plural 'staffs' nahi hota — 'his staff'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: As we are told, (1)/ this idea of Mrs. Gandhi's (2)/ was totally valid in the (3)/ present condition of India. (4)
solution: No error. 'this idea of Mrs. Gandhi's' double possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: It is true (1)/ that I met (2)/ the Chief Minister (3)/ at one of my friend's houses. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'one of my friends' houses' — plural possessive 'friends'' chahiye.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The teacher said (1)/ to us that one ought (2)/ to work hard (3)/ in order to attain one goal. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'one' ke saath 'one's' — 'to attain one's goal'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Being sure of his (1)/ success, he told his friends that (2)/ he would never forget (3)/ even his enemies names. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: plural possessive — 'his enemies' names'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The survey gives (1)/ us much knowledge of the (2)/ public's opinion (3)/ on the matter of terrorism. (4)
solution: No error. 'the public's opinion' possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Very few soldiers (1)/ dared to turn (2)/ a deaf ear to their (3)/ Commander's-in-Chief orders. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: compound noun mein 's ant mein lagta hai — 'Commander-in-Chief's orders'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Your son's-in-law's friend (1)/ is seriously ill (2)/ and he wants you (3)/ to see him as soon as possible. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: compound noun mein 's sirf ant mein — 'son-in-law's friend'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I appreciate you (1)/ helping the poor who are really the (2)/ most ignored and completely deprived (3)/ section of our society. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: gerund 'helping' se pehle possessive — 'I appreciate your helping'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I have read (1)/ many plays of Shaw's (2)/ who is called the (3)/ Shakespeare of our age. (4)
solution: No error. 'many plays of Shaw's' double possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I do not want (1)/ to bring her to my home (2)/ because her rashness (3)/ is quite intolerable. (4)
solution: No error.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He is chiefly concerned (1)/ with his own problems (2)/ but you say (3)/ that his is not. (4)
solution: No error. Possessive pronoun 'his' akela use sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: A friend of my wife (1)/ came to me (2)/ yesterday and asked me for (3)/ some money. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: double possessive chahiye — 'A friend of my wife's'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Unfortunately for me (1)/ the root cause of my frustration (2)/ lies in the Governments (3)/ dubious policy. (4)
solution: Part (3) galat: possessive apostrophe missing — 'the Government's dubious policy'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He suggested to me (1)/ that I should go to the nursery (2)/ to bring some beautiful (3)/ summer's flowers. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: 'summer' yahan attributive noun hai — 'summer flowers' (no 's).
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He came to his wits end (1)/ when he found (2)/ that his younger brother (3)/ had slapped his wife. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: idiom mein apostrophe chahiye — 'at his wit's end'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: When Jaya and Shyama (1)/ came across Jyoti, they (2)/ caught one another hands (3)/ and talked for a while. (4)
solution: Part (3) galat: possessive chahiye — 'caught one another's hands'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The former P. M. of India's (1)/ Mr. P. V. Narsimha Rao's (2)/ visit to Japan strengthened the relationship between (3)/ India and Japan. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: appositive mein 's sirf last noun par — 'of India Mr. P. V. Narsimha Rao's visit'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: It was (1)/ India's past (2)/ glory that attracted (3)/ a number of invaders in the remote past. (4)
solution: No error. 'India's past glory' sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Everybody should be careful (1)/ to fulfil the society's (2)/ demands and thereby show his (3)/ attachment to it. (4)
solution: No error. 'the society's demands' possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: 'Everybody's business (1)/ is nobody's business', (2)/ said the teacher (3)/ to the students. (4)
solution: No error. 'Everybody's' aur 'nobody's' dono possessive sahi hain.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: I postponed him (1)/ going to Delhi tonight as (2)/ I wanted him to deal with (3)/ one of my major problems. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: gerund 'going' se pehle possessive — 'I postponed his going'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He told her (1)/ that he had ever seen (2)/ her with his (3)/ mind's eye. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: affirmative sentence mein 'ever' ki jagah 'never' — 'had never seen'. ('mind's eye' sahi hai.)
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: When I arrived (1)/ at the gate of his house, (2)/ I found his (3)/ locking up the door. (4)
solution: Part (3) galat: perception verb 'found' ke baad objective case — 'I found him locking up the door'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: India and China's present (1)/ condition clearly indicate (2)/ that both the countries (3)/ will face a lot of problems in the coming time. (4)
solution: Part (2) galat: subject 'condition' singular hai — 'indicates'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Keeping in view (1)/ the public's demands, (2)/ the company has declared to improve the quality and lower (3)/ the prices of its products. (4)
solution: Part (3) galat: 'declared to improve' unidiomatic hai — 'decided to improve'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: His maid servant (1)/ behaviour is undoubtedly indecent (2)/ but I don't (3)/ understand how he tolerates it. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: possessive apostrophe missing — 'maid servant's behaviour'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: These buildings decoration (1)/ is not as much (2)/ impressive as it (3)/ ought to be. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: plural possessive — 'These buildings' decoration'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Delhi's water supply (1)/ system is certainly as good (2)/ as is expected (3)/ in a capital town. (4)
solution: No error. 'Delhi's water supply system' sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Yeats's poetry is characterised (1)/ by his unique style (2)/ which he developed (3)/ during the long span of his life. (4)
solution: No error. 'Yeats's' (singular proper noun + 's) sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Brutus role in 'Julius Ceasure' (1)/ is very attractive as (2)/ well as important as far as (3)/ the action of the play is concerned. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: possessive chahiye — 'Brutus's role' (ya 'Brutus' role').
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The blind's life (1)/ is really very miserable (2)/ because they (3)/ can't see what happens around them. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: 'the blind' plural class hai — 'The life of the blind' hona chahiye.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: He did not know even (1)/ the name of the scientist who (2)/ discovered (3)/ the laws of the earth gravitational force. (4)
solution: Part (4) galat: possessive chahiye — 'the earth's gravitational force'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: This blade's edge (1)/ is sharper than any (2)/ other blade's, (3)/ said the sales representative of the company. (4)
solution: No error. 'other blade's' (= other blade's edge) elliptical possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Many of the questions' (1)/ given in this question paper (2)/ are so difficult that (3)/ many of the students may fail. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: yahan plural chahiye, possessive nahi — 'Many of the questions'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: The President of India (1)/ constitutional power is limitless (2)/ but it is the P.M. who is, (3)/ indeed, powerful. (4)
solution: Part (1) galat: possessive missing — 'The President of India's constitutional power'.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Many a man's life is in danger (1)/ if the Government (2)/ does not take (3)/ any serious action to safeguard us. (4)
solution: No error. 'Many a man' singular hota hai — 'man's life' aur singular verb sahi hain.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases

question: Nobody's claim (1)/ to be a permanent member (2)/ of the club is valid (3)/ in the eyes of the committee. (4)
solution: No error. 'Nobody's claim' possessive sahi hai.
source: A Mirror of Common Errors · Nouns & Cases
